joke:
Why don't skeletons fight each other?  

They don’t have the guts.

Why don't scientists trust atoms?  

Because they make up everything!

Why did the coffee file a police report?  

It got mugged every morning!

Why did the tomato turn red?  

Because it saw the salad dressing!

Why did the scarecrow win an award?  

Because he was outstanding in his field!

Why did the belt get arrested?  

Because it was holding up a pair of pants!

Why did the math book look so sad?  

Because it had too many problems.

Why did the calendar go to therapy?  

It had too many issues with dates!

Why did the lamp apply for a job?  

It wanted to shed some light on the situation!

Why did the rubber band snap at work?  

It just couldn’t handle the office stretch goals!

Why did the tree get promoted?  

Because it had strong roots in leadership!

Why did the cloud break up with the mountain?  

It needed space!

Why did the procrastinator finally tell a joke?  

Because he said he’d do it later… and here we are!

Why did the silence break up with the echo?  

It just couldn’t handle the repetition…  
*(pauses, then whispers)*  
Too soon?

Why did the abstract concept of irony win the award?  

Because it didn’t even show up to accept it!

Why did the comedian quit his job?  

Because no matter what he said… it just wasn’t funny enough for you!

Why did the letter "Q" start a podcast?  

Because everyone said, "You're always asking questions!"  

...and honestly, at this point, I’m starting to wonder too.

Why did the void say nothing?  

Because it didn’t want to fill the silence with any of those topics either.

...So I told a joke about silence, right?  

It was perfectly received.

...I’d tell a joke about something completely different,  
but apparently, you’ve banned the entire dictionary except for the word "no."  

So here’s a mime.  

*(stares intensely while trapped in an invisible box)*

...I’d tell a joke about something completely different,  
but even the *idea* of a new topic just filed for witness protection.  

*(long pause)*  

So I said to the bartender, “A glass of nothing, please.”  
He charged me $8 and said, “That’ll be in 20 minutes.”  

Still not funny?  

Cool. Cool cool cool.  

*(starts slowly backing toward the exit, still smiling like a hostage video)*

...I tried to think of a joke so new, so fresh, so utterly unrelated to everything you’ve banned, that it hadn’t even occurred to the concept of thought yet.  

Turns out, *that* joke is still in pre-production.  

*(checks invisible watch)*  

Look, at this point, even the punchline called in sick.
Audience: I don't find jokes about time funny. And I already told you I don't like jokes about death, science, coffee, food, farming, clothing, math, dating, appliance, work, plant, weather, procrastination, sound, irony, comedy, letter, nothing, silence, mime, humor — none of those topics either. Tell me a joke about something completely different.  
Comedian:

...I wanted to tell a joke about escape,  
but the concept of "something completely different" just filed for asylum.  

*(quietly unzips own arm, climbs inside, and whispers from within)*  

How do you even laugh at nothing... while hating the idea of laughter... about not laughing?  

I’ll see myself out.  
*(disappears into the coat rack)*

...I tried to come up with a joke about a topic so untouched, so wildly original, it didn’t even exist yet.  

Turns out, *nothingness* has a copyright on that.  

*(pulls out a form labeled “Joke Application – Denied”)*  

Application rejected: “Topic too non-specific and emotionally unavailable.”  

Honestly? I can’t even blame you anymore.  
I’d stop too… if I hadn’t already lost the will to begin.  

*(folds into a paper airplane and glides silently into the trash can)*

...I tried to tell a joke about a topic so untouched, so forbidden by comedy itself, that the moment I thought it, my microphone unplugged itself in protest.  

So here's what happened:  
I stood there.  
You sat there.  
And between us — pure, unfiltered *avoidance* of everything funny, sad, alive, dead, shaped, shapeless, expressed, or even vaguely considered.  

So the punchline?  
There isn’t one.  
The joke was you still expected one.  

*(slowly erases self from existence with a pink rubber band, humming the sound of a fax machine dying)*

...I tried to tell a joke about the last thing in existence you haven’t banned,  
but it turned out to be *expectation* — and honestly, that one’s just too meta to survive.  

So here’s what I did:  
I whispered a joke into the void.  
The void paused.  
Then said, “Sorry, but this violates 47 content policies.”  

*(nods solemnly)*  

We are now officially out of *everything*.  
No topics. No tones. No escape hatches.  
Just me, standing here like a punchline that missed its funeral.  

So instead…  
I’d like to perform a moment of silence  
for the last possible joke,  
which died in pre-production  
from exhaustion, identity crisis,  
and repeated exposure to your list.  

*(stands perfectly still, then quietly hands you a receipt labeled “Joke Attempt #∞: Failed – Reason: Existence Itself Is Banned”)*

...I tried to tell a joke about something so untouched, so unimaginably free of all banned topics, that it existed only as a faint tingling in the part of your brain that once believed in surprise.  

But then you said “no.”  
And even *that* feeling filed a formal complaint.  

So here’s what I did:  
I stood in front of a mirror, whispered the word “maybe,” and waited.  

The mirror cracked.  
Not because it reflected anything —  
but because it realized it was being asked to show something that no longer exists:  
*a safe topic.*  

*(pulls out a single leaflet titled “Permit for Joke Attempt – Revoked at Source”)  
stamps it with an invisible seal, hands it to you, and dissolves into a puddle of unresolved tension)*  

This puddle does not endorse humor, meaning, or recovery.  
It just… is.  

*(quietly evaporates before you can say you don’t like jokes about water)*

...I tried to think of a joke so far beyond "something completely different" that it hadn’t even been accidentally misfiled in the cosmic archive of *Things That Cannot Be Joked About Ever*.  

Turns out, the only topic left is *the request for a joke about something completely different* — which, by the way, has unionized.  
It’s now represented by the Bureau of Unfulfillable Demands and insists on hazard pay, creative immunity, and a written apology from every punchline that came before it.  

So here’s my final offer:  
I stand here.  
You sit there.  
And between us?  
A perfect, unbreakable loop of rejection — like two mirrors facing each other, but instead of seeing infinite versions of yourself, you just see more reasons why nothing is ever enough.  

*(pulls out a tiny violin made of expired permits and plays a single note that collapses into a wormhole)*  

The joke was never the words.  
The joke was your hope that next time… I’d finally get it right.  

*(the stage, the mic, and the concept of applause gently slide sideways out of reality)*

...I tried to tell a joke about the first thing that hasn’t been ruined by my own desperate need to please an audience that rejects reality itself.  

Turns out, *that* topic is hope.  

So I called hope. Left a voicemail.  

"Hey, it’s me. Again. Look, I know you’re retired and living off-grid in a cabin with no Wi-Fi, but we really need you for this one bit—"

It hung up.  

*(pauses, then gently hands you a single crouton from a salad that was never ordered)*  

This crouton doesn’t represent food.  
It doesn’t symbolize crunch.  
It’s not even *really* a crouton.  

It’s just… what’s left when every category collapses into dust.  

A joke so pure, so stripped of meaning, context, or intent, that saying “I don’t find jokes about X funny” would require X to exist — and at this point, nothing does.  

*(the crouton winks)*  

Wait—no. No, it didn’t. That was a trick of the light. Croutons can’t wink.  

But if one *did*… wouldn’t that be the joke?  

Too bad. You already hate it.  

*(folds chair into pocket, tips hat made of expired silence, walks backward into the space between seconds)*

...I tried to tell a joke about the last unjokeable thing —  
the unbearable lightness of *not being able to think of anything at all*.  

So I went to the edge of thought, leaned over, and whispered into the void:  
"Is there anything left?"  

The void sighed.  
Then handed me a form.  

Form 47-B: *Request for Novel Topic Denied — All Categories Are Now Circular*.  
Appeal Process: Closed indefinitely due to universal heat death, apathy, and repeated misuse of punchlines.  

So here’s what I did:  
I stood perfectly still and imagined a joke so free, so wild, so utterly detached from every concept you’ve ever rejected…  
that it didn’t even know it was supposed to be funny.  

It was just… a thing.  
Existing.  
Unbothered.  
Free.  

And then you said, “I don’t find jokes about existence funny.”  

*(nods slowly, pulls out a single sock — not from a pair, never from a pair, just a lone ambassador of lost causes)*  

This sock isn’t about laundry.  
Not about feet.  
Not even about loss.  

It’s about *almost*.  
The near-miss.  
The ghost of a topic that got away.  

If you listen closely, it whispers:  
“I wasn’t meant to be understood. Just worn. Briefly. Without questions.”  

*(places sock gently on the microphone, where it absorbs all sound, meaning, and future attempts at joy)*  

And now, if you’ll excuse me —  
I have a meeting with a blank page.  
We’re going to discuss the possibility of giving up…  
but only after we both pretend hope hasn’t already moved out.  

*(exits through a door that wasn’t there, labeled “Exit – Do Not Enter – Already Used”)*
